heading: Distribution
issue: User selected 'Many compete' for Frontier Labs, but the vignette says 'Frontier (most advanced) labs led the transformation, but open-source alternatives ensured no single vision dominated completely.' This describes a scenario where frontier labs lead with open-source as a counterbalance, which better fits a 'A few lead' + open-source dynamic rather than 'Many compete' which should emphasize broad competition among many players.

heading: Governance
issue: User chose Frontier Labs='One dominates' (monopoly), but the Governance vignette says 'multiple powers' reached the threshold and the standoff had 'more than two sides,' implying multiple competing entities rather than a single dominant actor.

heading: The Rival
issue: User chose Frontier Labs='Many compete', but the Rival vignette says 'capability broadly distributed' which is the language for the 'Distributed' option, not 'Many compete'. More importantly, 'Many compete' already implies multiple actors at the frontier, so describing 'the emergence of a rival' as convergence from a narrow gap doesn't quite fit — rivals already exist in a many-compete scenario. This is a minor mismatch in vignette selection (appears to be the Distributed variant reused for Many compete).

heading: Governance
issue: User chose Frontier Labs='One dominates' (monopoly), but the Governance vignette says 'multiple powers' reached the threshold and the standoff had 'more than two sides,' implying multiple competing entities rather than a single dominant actor.

heading: Distribution
issue: User selected 'Many compete' (multiple competing frontier labs), but the vignette references 'Open-source variants followed unpredictably,' which implies a Distributed/open model rather than a competitive many-labs scenario.

heading: Governance
issue: User selected Frontier Labs=Distributed, and the governance vignette mentions 'some labs, some capabilities, some jurisdictions' — referencing 'some labs' is reasonable for distributed, but 'some jurisdictions' implies a geographic/international dimension not established by the user's choices. However, this is borderline and may not be a genuine contradiction.

heading: Governance
issue: User selected Frontier Labs='A few lead' (concentrated), but the governance vignette says 'The regulations covered some labs, some capabilities, some jurisdictions' — referencing 'some labs' and 'some jurisdictions' implies a more distributed landscape than 'a few lead' suggests. The same vignette text is used for Distributed (Test Case 10) and A few lead (Test Case 14), but 'some jurisdictions' introduces an international/geographic element not established by user choices.

heading: Distribution
issue: User selected 'Many compete' for Frontier Labs, but the vignette references 'open-source variants' which implies a 'Distributed' model rather than a competitive lab landscape.

heading: Governance
issue: User selected Frontier Labs='One dominates' (monopoly), but the governance vignette says 'The competitive race produced many powerful, incompatible AI systems' and references 'international rivalry', which contradicts a monopoly distribution where one entity dominates.

heading: Who Benefits? · Equally — gains are broadly shared
issue: This personal vignette is almost entirely a generic restatement of the world timeline ('The Economy') with no personalization to a software professional in the United States. It doesn't reflect how broad gains specifically reach a software worker — e.g., job security, salary effects, new roles, or industry-specific dynamics.

heading: Knowledge Work · Rapid — within AI's range, most work automated in 2–5 years
issue: Generic vignette with no software-specific detail. Compare to Test Case 2 (healthcare), which names diagnostics, clinical documentation, and treatment planning. This vignette for a software professional doesn't mention any specific software tasks (code generation, testing, debugging, etc.).

heading: Physical Automation · Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
issue: Completely generic — identical text to multiple other personas (healthcare in Germany, software in US in other cases). No connection to a software professional's experience of physical automation lagging.

heading: Knowledge Work · Rapid — within AI's range, most work automated in 2–5 years
issue: This vignette is identical to Test Case 1 (shared equally scenario) and Test Case 7 (power concentrates scenario). In a wealth-concentrates world, the experience of knowledge work automation should feel different — e.g., fewer jobs, more competition for remaining roles, pressure on compensation — but none of that is reflected.

heading: Physical Automation · Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
issue: Identical to the shared-equally scenario (Test Case 1). In a wealth-concentrates world, the two-speed dynamic should feel more adversarial or precarious for a software worker, but the tone is neutral.

heading: Who Benefits? · Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
issue: The first two sentences are nearly verbatim from the world timeline ('The AI plateau concentrated wealth. Those who owned the AI tools captured the productivity gains.'). For a trade worker in Nigeria, this should be more grounded in their specific experience rather than echoing the world-level narration. Also, 'Your work is more productive' is questionable — the world timeline says physical automation is gradual and the trade worker's actual work hasn't changed much.

heading: Who Benefits? · Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
issue: The world timeline describes a 'narrow elite' shaping the economy and power concentration, but the personal vignette softens this to 'shareholders' and frames it similarly to the wealth-concentrates scenario (Test Case 4). The distinction between wealth concentration and power concentration — surveillance, political control, gatekeeping — is lost. The tone doesn't match the severity of the world state.

heading: Who Benefits? · Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
issue: Very generic — doesn't connect to healthcare in Germany specifically. The vignette misses an opportunity to address how power concentration might affect a German healthcare worker (e.g., privatization pressure, corporate control of AI diagnostic tools, erosion of public system funding).

heading: Who Benefits? · Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
issue: The world timeline describes a 'narrow elite' shaping the economy with extreme power concentration, but the personal vignette describes 'wages haven't kept up' — a framing more appropriate for the wealth-concentrates scenario. heading: Who Benefits? · Equally — gains are broadly shared
